retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Uterine Bleeding -- Evaluation -- Imaging Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Saline-infused sonohysterography may be considered to enhance the detection of intracavitary lesions by introducing sterile saline into the uterine cavity during TVUS; however, this method is not as useful for endometrial assessment if the endometrium was not well visualized on initial ultrasound. [12] Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is typically reserved for cases where TVUS is inadequate, eg, patients with complex anatomy, leiomyomas, or adenomyosis. Diffusion-weighted imaging can improve diagnostic accuracy, particularly for differentiating benign and malignant lesions. [12] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Uterine Imaging -- Magnetic Resonance
retrieved_docs.content: MRI is the modality of choice given the high tissue spatial resolution and absence of ionizing radiation. The different layers of the uterus are distinctly visualized. The junctional zone can experience thickening in adenomyosis. The advantage of MRI is that it can also evaluate the adjacent structures, such as the ovaries, fallopian tubes, urinary bladder, and rectum. Deep pelvic lymph nodes, which are not well visualized on ultrasound, can also be assessed on MRI. Multiple sequences in different planes are obtained. Most common sequences include a sagittal T2-weighted image, coronal and axial T2, sagittal and or axial fat-saturated T2-weighted sequence, angled axials to evaluate the cervix and Mullerian duct anomalies, axial T1 weighted sequence including in and out of phase sequence, diffusion-weighted sequences, and dynamic sequences for evaluating the enhancement characteristics of the various uterine lesions. [3] Restricted diffusion sequences are invaluable in assessing mass lesions within and around the uterus.

retrieved_docs.title: Appropriate Magnetic Resonance Imaging Ordering -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: A small study of 81 female patients with sonographically indeterminate pelvic masses showed that even limited sequence MR could define uterine vs. extra-uterine origins of a mass, determining and guiding clinical management. [15]

retrieved_docs.content: AdnexMR 2 : Characteristics of these lesions are considered benign. The established features of this category include purely adnexal cystic, endometriotic, or fatty masses with no wall enhancement or solid tissue or solid masses with a low homogenous signal on the T2-weighted and the high b-value diffusion-weighted images.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- Evaluation -- MRI
retrieved_docs.content: Similar to ultrasound, a variety of mimics ranging from co-existing gynecologic pathologies to physiologic variants exist. Susceptibility weighted imaging, diffusion-weighted imaging, MR spectroscopy, cine MR imaging, and increased 3T field strength are all problem-solving strategies. [18] It is important to obtain the MRI in the late proliferative or secretory phase (days 7 to 28) due to the decreased signal of normal myometrium during the early proliferative phase (days 1 to 6). [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Postmenopausal Bleeding -- Evaluation -- Imaging Studies
retrieved_docs.content: A thickened endometrial stripe may be caused by hyperplasia, malignancy, and intracavitary lesions (eg, leiomyomas and endometrial polyps). For ultrasound findings suggestive of intracavitary lesions or history-indicated suspicion (eg, previous polyps), additional imaging may be useful, including saline-infused ultrasonography or hysterosalpingogram. Pelvic computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) are sometimes used to characterize better urogenital pathology noted on ultrasound. [4] However, hysteroscopy with dilation and curettage is the gold standard as diagnostic sampling and therapeutic excision for some etiologies of PMB (eg, endometrial polyps) can be performed simultaneously. Furthermore, blind endometrial sampling without hysteroscopy may miss focal lesions or intrauterine pathology, such as polyps, and mass lesions may deflect flexible disposable devices. Consequently, further imaging evaluation should be considered for patients with insufficient sampling or persistent vaginal bleeding in whom focal lesions may have been missed; hysteroscopy with dilation and curettage or directed biopsy may be warranted. [5] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Adenomyosis -- Evaluation -- MRI
retrieved_docs.content: Characteristic findings on MRI parallel the same features seen on ultrasound: [18] [19] On T2-weighted imaging, uterine enlargement characterized by ill-defined, low-signal-intensity regions within the junctional zone is reflective of smooth muscle hyperplasia (junctional zone thicker than 12 mm is generally accepted as diagnostic) T2 hyperintense myometrial cysts reflecting regions of ectopic endometrial tissue (can also have increased intrinsic T1 signal or increased susceptibility in hemorrhagic foci) Contrast enhancement is generally not reliable for assessment of vascularity as compared to color Doppler ultrasound

retrieved_docs.content: The recommended technique for MRI consists of precontrast axial T1-weighted and T2-weighted images with and without fat suppression, dynamic sequence postcontrast T1-weighted images, followed by perfusion-weighted and diffusion-weighted sequences if an adnexal mass was visualized with T1-weighted and T2-weighted images. A slice less than or equal to 3 mm thickness for the T2-weighted and contrast-enhanced T1-weighted images is recommended. retrieved_docs.title: Uterine Imaging -- Nuclear Medicine
retrieved_docs.content: PET-CT is useful in evaluating gynecological malignancies. Though MRI is superior and accurate for local extension and staging of the disease, distant metastasis in gynecological malignancies can be better assessed with PET-CT. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Uterine Imaging -- Clinical Significance -- Fibroids
retrieved_docs.content: The presence of ectopic endometrial tissue within the myometrium is adenomyosis. Ultrasound and MRI are both useful for evaluating adenomyosis. On ultrasound, findings suggest adenomyosis includes heterogeneous myometrium—nonspecific but indicative of adenomyosis. This finding is termed pencil shadowing due to the proliferation of endometrial glands within the myometrium.MRI is a slightly more sensitive study for evaluating adenomyosis. Features on MRI suggestive of adenomyosis include: Thickening of the junctional zone beyond 12 mm The presence of tiny T2 bright foci within the myometrium indicates endometrial glands growing into the myometrium Focal adenomyosis, which presents as foci of ill-defined decreased T2 signal within the myometrium

retrieved_docs.content: In conclusion, the uterus can be imaged using multiple modalities. However, ultrasound and MRI are the most commonly used modalities in uterine imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: Postmenopausal Bleeding -- Evaluation -- Imaging Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Transvaginal imaging to measure endometrial thickness may also be used as a first-line modality to evaluate PMB in patients without indications for histologic assessment. The technique ACOG recommends to measure endometrial thickness consists of using the thickest portion of the endometrial stripe seen on a long-axis uterine view, measuring anterior to posterior. [5] An endometrial stripe thickness of ≤4 mm has a negative predictive value >99% for endometrial carcinoma. [35] The probability of having endometrial cancer with an endometrial stripe thickness of <4 mm is approximately 0.3%. Using this 4 mm threshold, only 1 in 339 endometrial cancers are missed, according to studies. [5] Ultrasound imaging may also identify other pathologic pelvic etiologies (eg, leiomyomas or adnexal masses). However, further evaluation with endometrial sampling is indicated if the endometrial stripe is inadequately visualized. Because endometrial imaging is not diagnostic and endometrial thickness does not always exclude malignancy, clinicians should perform an endometrial biopsy for continued or recurrent PMB, even for patients with an endometrial lining <4 mm. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1812
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine/endometrial disorders are classified as a subset within the broader category of enlarged uterine leiomyoma tumors (أورام ليفية رحمية متضخمة), indicating that conditions affecting the uterus and endometrium are encompassed by the pathology of large fibroid growths. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing fibroid enlargement as a central component in the spectrum of uterine/endometrial disease and suggests that assessment of fibroid size and impact is a key aspect of managing patients with uterine/endometrial disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1579
retrieved_docs.content: Endometrial sampling (biopsy) with or without endocervical sampling (biopsy), without cervical dilation, any method (separate procedure) is a diagnostic procedure that falls within the specialty of physicians practicing obstetrics and gynecology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2692
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is recognized as an allowable therapeutic option for both female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and uterine endometriosis (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). These two gynecologic conditions are interrelated, as uterine endometriosis is a known contributor to infertility in women. Consequently, radiotherapy may be considered within the broader management strategies for these linked disorders, reflecting their clinical connection and the potential role of radiotherapeutic interventions in addressing both conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1853
retrieved_docs.content: Other diagnostic procedures on cervix are classified within the broader category of other diagnostic procedures; female organs. This overarching group of female‑organ diagnostic interventions includes procedures such as تنظيم جوف الحوض (pelvic endoscopy), خزعة المهبل (vaginal biopsy), and منظار الرحم (uterine hysteroscopy). Together, these procedures represent specialized diagnostic techniques used to evaluate the cervix, vagina, uterus, and surrounding pelvic structures in female patients, facilitating accurate assessment and management of gynecologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is a broad clinical category that includes a range of conditions affecting the female reproductive organs. Within this category, cancer of the uterus represents a malignant neoplasm of the uterine tissue, while ورم مهبلي حميد (benign vaginal tumor) denotes a non‑malignant growth in the vagina. Recognizing both uterine cancer and benign vaginal tumors as specific manifestations of Female Reproductive System Disorder highlights their shared relevance to gynecologic health and underscores the importance of tailored diagnostic and therapeutic strategies for each condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 452
retrieved_docs.content: منظار المهبل (vaginal endoscopy), indicating that minimally invasive visualization is often employed in the diagnostic and therapeutic work‑up of early‑stage cervical lesions. منظار المهبل is further linked as a child of إجراءات تصوير متعلقة بالأعضاء التناسلية والثدي (imaging procedures related to genital and breast organs), highlighting its role within a broader imaging framework. Both خلل تنسج عنق الرحم and سرطان عنق الرحم المرحلة 0 are members of the malignant tumor group أورام الرحم وقناة فالوب الخبيثة (SMQ) , connecting these early cervical pathologies to the larger classification of uterine and fallopian tube cancers. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how various forms of abdominal and radical hysterectomy, often combined with ovarian surgery, are integral to the management of cervical intra‑epithelial neoplasia, early cervical cancer, and related stromal disorders, and how they are supported by specific imaging techniques within comprehensive gynecologic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1402
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine smooth muscle tumor (ورم عضلي أملس رحمي) is a gynecologic condition that is classified as a benign neoplasm of the uterus and originates from female internal genitalia structures. It is synonymous with enlarged uterine fibroid tumors (أورام ليفية رحمية متضخمة), which are a subcategory of uterine/endometrial disorders. Clinically, an unknown type of uterine scar (UTERINE SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN) is associated with this tumor. Understanding these interconnections clarifies the tumor’s classification, anatomical origin, related disorder categories, and clinical associations, which is essential for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1644
retrieved_docs.content: Open biopsy of uterus is directly mapped to the Arabic term خزعة بطانة الرحم, which denotes an endometrial biopsy. The same Arabic term is also linked to diagnostic dilatation and curettage (D&C) as an alternative or related procedure, indicating that diagnostic D&C can be considered another method associated with خزعة بطانة الرحم. Both procedures involve sampling uterine tissue for diagnostic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1091
retrieved_docs.content: encompasses organ‑specific biopsies (ovarian and uterine), the uterine biopsy further subdivides into endometrial biopsy, and all are situated within the broader histopathology framework overseen by obstetrics‑gynecology specialists, who also perform related procedures such as salpingo‑oophorectomy and endometrial sampling. This integrated structure aids clinicians in understanding procedural categorization, appropriate specialist involvement, and the interconnections among related diagnostic and therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2543
retrieved_docs.content: Other diagnostic procedures on cervix are categorized under the broader group of Other diagnostic procedures; female organs. The category Other diagnostic procedures; female organs encompasses specific diagnostic techniques such as تنظير جوف الحوض (pelvic cavity endoscopy) and منظار الرحم (uterine endoscopy). These procedures are employed to evaluate and diagnose conditions affecting female pelvic structures, including the cervix, uterus, and surrounding pelvic cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 992
retrieved_docs.content: This integrated framework aids clinicians in understanding the interconnections among various gynecologic conditions, facilitating more precise diagnosis, classification, and management of patients with disorders of the female reproductive tract.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2604
retrieved_docs.content: Endometrial sampling (biopsy) with or without endocervical sampling, performed without cervical dilation by any method as a separate procedure, and excision of the cervical stump by an abdominal approach are both clinical procedures that belong to the field of obstetrics and gynecology. Physicians specializing in obstetrics and gynecology are the providers who perform these interventions, reflecting the scope of surgical and diagnostic techniques within this specialty.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2486
retrieved_docs.content: Endocervical biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on the cervix are both classified as “Other diagnostic procedures; female organs.” This category of female‑organ diagnostic procedures is itself classified under the broader modality of pelvic endoscopy (تنظير جوف الحوض). Consequently, endocervical biopsy and similar cervical diagnostic techniques are understood to be specialized forms of pelvic endoscopic procedures used in the evaluation of female patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 662
retrieved_docs.content: “Other excision of cervix and uterus” is clinically linked to cervical stromal disorder (خلل تنسج عنق الرحم) and co‑occurs with stage 0 cervical cancer and vaginal endoscopy (منظار المهبل), highlighting its relevance to early cervical neoplasia management. This hierarchical and co‑occurrence information supports accurate coding, epidemiologic tracking, and coordinated clinical care of gynecologic surgical and oncologic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1944
